title: Structured Output and Tool Use — Check your understanding

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using tool use over prompted JSON for structured extraction?
options: Tool use is faster because it skips the text generation step
Tool use costs fewer tokens than generating JSON text
The API enforces the schema, so you get typed arguments without parsing or markdown stripping
Tool use allows the model to search the web before returning results
questions.explanation: With prompted JSON, the model may wrap output in markdown, add explanation, or format fields inconsistently — you parse and hope. With tool use, the API validates the model's output against your schema before returning it. You receive a typed dict, not a string.

questions.question: You want the model to always call a specific tool called 'classify_ticket'. What should you set in the API request?
options: Set temperature to 0 so the model makes deterministic choices
Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "classify_ticket"}
List only that tool in the tools array so the model has no other option
Add 'You must use the classify_ticket tool' to the system prompt
questions.explanation: tool_choice forces a specific tool call at the API level. Listing only one tool is also effective, but tool_choice is the explicit, reliable mechanism. System prompt instructions alone are not enforced at the API level and can be ignored.

questions.question: Your tool-use loop runs but never terminates. The model keeps calling tools without producing a final answer. What is the most likely cause and the correct fix?
options: The model is too large — switch to a smaller model that terminates faster
The tools are returning errors — fix the tools so they always succeed
The loop is not checking stop_reason, and there is no maximum turn limit
tool_choice is set incorrectly — remove it so the model can decide when to stop
questions.explanation: An infinite tool loop almost always means stop_reason is not being checked, or there is no turn limit. When stop_reason is 'end_turn', the model has finished — return the text response and exit the loop. Always add a maximum turn limit (e.g. 20) as a safety net for ambiguous tasks or misbehaving tools.

questions.question: You have a tool with a 'priority' field that accepts values low, medium, or high. What schema feature best prevents the model from inventing unexpected values like 'urgent' or 'critical'?
options: Set the field type to 'integer' and document the mapping in the description
Use an enum constraint listing the allowed values
Add a system prompt instruction listing the valid priorities
Mark the field as required so the model cannot skip it
questions.explanation: An enum in the JSON schema constrains the output space to exactly the values you specify. The model cannot generate values outside the enum. System prompt instructions can be missed; required only ensures the field is present, not what value it has.

questions.question: Your application has 40 registered tools but any given user request only needs 2–3 of them. What is the production concern?
options: The model will randomly pick tools it was not asked to use
Tools registered but not called still count against the rate limit
All 40 tool definitions are sent with every request, consuming context window tokens and increasing cost
The API will reject requests with more than 20 tools
questions.explanation: Tool definitions are included in the request payload and count against the context window. 40 detailed tool definitions can add thousands of tokens to every call — even when 37 of them are irrelevant. The fix is to select only the tools relevant to the current task before sending the request.
